question: In a child with central diabetes insipidus, which of the following statements is true regarding the diagnosis and treatment?
options: A. A low copeptin concentration would establish the diagnosis of DI of any cause
B. A water-deprivation study is indicated but should be done on an inpatient basis
C. Therapy with desmopressin can be initiated
D. Serum and urine osmolality should be measured
explanation: The correct answer is C: Therapy with desmopressin can be initiated. This child has a calculated serum osmolality already above 304 mOsm/kg (twice the sodium), plus the additional component from the glucose (5 mOsm/kg). This finding, coupled with the urine, establishes the diagnosis of DI. A low copeptin concentration in the face of hyperosmolality might imply central DI (Answer A); however, this remains to be proven in children. In addition, it would be a test only for central DI, not nephrogenic DI.
reference: MAIN CONCLUSIONS: "The main treatment is desmopressin. Desmopressin was introduced in the 1970s and remains the main treatment. Desmopressin is a synthetic analogue of the hormone 8-arginine AVP and has a longer half-life than AVP. The antidiuretic action is more specific than that of AVP, and it has less pressor activity."
title: diabetes Insipidus in Children
